coredata.dc:title: Magnetic-field assisted synthesis of carbon dots-doped polyaniline nanotubes with a high-performance supercapacitance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jian
Rehman, Sajid ur
Wang, Jingmin
Fang, Zhenbin
Zhang, Xiang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots-doped polyaniline nanotubes (CDs@PANI-0.5 T) have been synthesized under an applied magnetic field (MF) of 0.5 T. Not only the applied MF has improved the morphology, crystallinity, and conductivity of PANI nanotubes, but also the doped CDs with large specific surface area increase the electron mobility and electron density of the PANI nanotubes. Which eventually reduce both the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and internal resistance (R s) of the electrode materials, and thus enhance the supercapacitance behavior of the electrodes. The supercapacitor assembled from CDs@PANI-0.5 T electrodes shows a superior specific capacitance, high energy density, and cycle stability to control samples of PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline
Carbon dots
Magnetic field
Supercapacitance
Nanotubes

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs-doped PANI nanotubes (CDs@PANI nanotubes)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CDs@PANI-0.5 T nanotubes (NTs), in particular, showing an electron transfer process from PANI to CDs.

SEM images of (a) CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs and (b) CDs@PANI-0 T; (c) Magnified SEM image of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs; (d) TEM image of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs, the inset shows a high resolution TEM image of an individual carbon dot on a nanotube.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, PANI-0 T, and CDs.

The electrochemical performances of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T samples in a three-electrode system: (a) GCD curves at a current density of 0.5 A g−1; (b) GCD curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T at different current densities; (c) CV curves at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1; (d) CV curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T detected at various scan rates; (e) I–V plots of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T samples; (f) Nyquist plots in the frequency range from 100 mHz to 100 kHz.
Dielectric permittivity (ε′) variations with frequency of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T and PANI-0 T.
(a) Preserved specific capacitances and (b) coulombic efficiency of electrodes in 2000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 10.0 A g−1.

The electrochemical performances of symmetric supercapacitors with CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T composites in a two-electrode system: (a) GCD curves at a current density of 0.5 A g−1; (b) GCD curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T at different current densities; (c) CV curves at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1; (d) CV curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T detected at various scan rates.
Ragone plots of the CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T samples.

head.ce:title: Magnetic-field assisted synthesis of carbon dots-doped polyaniline nanotubes with a high-performance supercapacitance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hefei Normal University, Hefei 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots-doped polyaniline nanotubes (CDs@PANI-0.5 T) have been synthesized under an applied magnetic field (MF) of 0.5 T. Not only the applied MF has improved the morphology, crystallinity, and conductivity of PANI nanotubes, but also the doped CDs with large specific surface area increase the electron mobility and electron density of the PANI nanotubes. Which eventually reduce both the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and internal resistance (R s) of the electrode materials, and thus enhance the supercapacitance behavior of the electrodes. The supercapacitor assembled from CDs@PANI-0.5 T electrodes shows a superior specific capacitance, high energy density, and cycle stability to control samples of PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T.

ce:section.ce:para: As an outstanding new-generation energy storage system, supercapacitor energy storage devices have received more and more attention due to their advantages of high specific capacitance, high energy density, and good cycle stability. In recent years, this energy storage device has been widely used in electric vehicles, portable electronic devices, wearable electronic devices, and so on. [1,2]. Compared to traditional batteries, supercapacitors are easy to prepare, safe, environment-friendly, lightweight, and energy-efficient. Which considered the most powerful competitors in the field of energy storage. [3]. However, the problem with supercapacitors is they attain low energy density and relatively low voltage, which keeps the barrier of wide-spread use of super capacitor in potential applications [4]. To improve the performance of supercapacitors, a new work focuses on the hollow structure and improve the conductivity of electrode has begun recently.

The charge storage capacity of the double layer capacitor mainly comes from the enrichment of ions and electrons between the electrode and electrolyte interface also effectively improves the charge storage capability of pseudocapacitor [5,6]. High specific capacitance is dependent on a large number of components, for example, effective surface area, electrode structure, adsorbed ion concentration, active sites for ion transport and most importantly the amount of pseudocapacitance [7,8]. Therefore, preparing conductive polymers under external magnetic fields is an effective way to improve their crystallinity and conductivity. In addition, it was found that the hollow structure not only improved the specific surface area and the specific capacitance but also improved the cycle stability of the electrode material [5,9].

To achieve high electrochemical performance, the active electrode material used for the supercapacitor should have a large electrolyte-accessible surface area and high electrical conductivity [6]. In recent years, while maintaining the maximum the energy density, the power density of supercapacitors was also desired to be increased [10,11]. Conductive polymer supercapacitors such as polythiophene (PTP), polypyrrole (PPy), and polyaniline (PANI) are of popular research materials because of their merits of highly conductive, low density, large specific capacitance and charge/discharge efficiency [12,13]. Among all, PANI has excellent electrical conductivity and large specific surface area, which is consider as the best candidate for supercapacitor electrode materials [14–16]. Polyaniline has been widely studied due to its low density, environmental friendliness, high specific capacitance, low density, and ease of preparing [17,18]. The research on PANI-based hybrid supercapacitors have been progressed rapidly in the last several years. However, there are still some issues that remain to be overcome to achieve the device performances, like increase in both specific power and energy densities, developing eco-friendly electrolyte systems, and synthesis of electrode materials with desirable electrical/mechanical properties for intelligent and wearable devices [19–21]. Therefore, improve the specific capacitance and power density of polyaniline supercapacitors, make them applicable to practical production is of great significance [22].

In order to improve the physical properties of PANI, different fabrication methods have been employed in past. By changing the experimental conditions such as doping, reaction temperature, reaction time, etc. have influenced on morphology and magnetic properties [23,24]. Among other physical parameters during growth of materials, external applied magnetic field (MF) is an important parameter. Compared to traditional synthesis, the sample prepared under MF will generate a new type of materials, because MF might be more effective than conventional preparation parameters [25,26]. The applied MF continuously transfer a uniform amount of energy to atoms and molecules through the lines of forces from north to south pole of magnetic field, causing the alignment of atoms, molecules, and dipoles in the direction of magnetic force. [27–30]. Apart from the improved crystallinity, the MF also can control the morphologies and specific surface area of samples [29]. Therefore, it expected to realize the morphology change and crystalline improvement in PANI and PANI-based materials using MF during synthesis.

As small carbon nanoparticles (<10 nm), carbon dots (CDs) including carbon nanodots and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are becoming new research hot topic in nearly fifteen years. Usually, they consist of a carbon core with sp2-hybridization and a plenty of functional groups at the surface [31,32]. CDs have extraordinary optical properties, can act as a good electron donor, and also a good electron acceptor [33]. Maity et al. prepared PANI-GQD composites, which improved the conductivity three times compared with GQDs, and they also found the charge transfer between conductive polymer and GQDs by the fluorescence quenching phenomenon [34]. Recently, our group has prepared a composite of CDs/PPy/GO and investigated the role of CDs in the enhancement of supercapacitance [35]. We found from our previous knowledge that doping of CDs had a great influence on the supercapacitor properties like high capacitance and energy density.

In this work, CDs-doped PANI nanotubes were prepared under a low static MF (0.5 T). The specific capacitance of the sample prepared under the MF achieves 609 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, and after charge-discharge for 2000 cycles, the capacitance can still keep 76.51% retention, which shows a much better cycle stability than the control sample obtained without MF.

Aniline (Ani), ammonium persulfate (APS), camphorsulfonic acid (CSA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. The food-grade konjac flour (KF) bought from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. Before use, aniline was distilled, APS and CSA have not been treated, the distilled water was used in the synthesis process.

According to the previous literature, the CDs were prepared by thermolysis method [36]. 3 g of KF powder was heated at 350 °C for 90 min in the air. Next, the resulting black solid was ground and the resulting fine powder was stirred with 100 ml of absolute ethanol for 6 h. CDs were extracted from the above solution through a 0.22 mm filter, and the resulting mixed solution dried at 60 °C for 24 h. After that, CDs re-dispersed and filtrated again to remove hydrophilic organic components and residual inorganic salts. Finally, after drying the filtrate, the yellow solid CDs powder was collected. The TEM image (Fig. S1) confirms that as-synthesized CDs are fairly uniform, and the narrow size distribution histogram shows it has an average diameter of about 2.46 nm [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs-doped PANI nanotubes (CDs@PANI nanotubes)
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , CDs@PANI nanotubes were synthesized under 0.5 T MF by in-situ polymerization. First of all, a uniform (homogeneous) solution was obtain by 0.2 g CDs, 0.93 g CSA and 2.23 g aniline and 500 mL deionized water, then the mixed solution stirring for 10 min at 5 °C. Next, keep the mixed solution under a 0.5 T MF for 1 h without stirring, and then add 500 mL mixture solution which contains 5.47 g APS. After stirring to make sure complete mixing, the reaction solution keep under a 0.5 T MF for 24 h at 5 °C. After reaction finished, washed the products with ethanol and deionize water repeatedly, finally dried it for overnight at 50 °C under vacuum. The obtain CDs@PANI nanotubes named as CDs@PANI-0.5 T A similar synthesis process to that of CDs@PANI hybrid without MF during the polymerization was done, and then the CDs@PANI with nanofiber morphology, namely, CDs@PANI-0 T was obtained as a control. In addition, the similar synthesis process synthesized PANI-0.5 T and PANI-0 T without CDs.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) has been employed for the morphology of samples. Raman spectra was characterized by a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectroscopy (λex = 532 nm). XRD patterns were recorded by an X'Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å), and the scanning rate was 2° min−1. The operation current and voltage maintained at 20 mA and 36 kV, respectively. The BET surface area data was recorded by N2 adsorption at −196 °C (Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer). Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) done by SDT Q600 from ambient temperature to 800 °C in N2 atmosphere at 10 °C min−1. The real permittivity (ε′) of the samples was measured by using an Agilent E5071C vector network analyzer in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz at room temperature.

The electrochemical measurement of samples were taken by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. In a three-electrode system, the cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were measured in 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The Nafion impregnated sample casting onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 4 mm) which was prepared as a working electrode. To keep the electrode sensitivity, it was polished with 0.03 μm α-alumina powder. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as reference electrodes, and platinum wire employed as counter electrodes. The EIS test was recorded from 100 mHz to 100 kHz and performed at 0 V (vs. SCE) using an AC amplitude of 5 mV. The potential windows of CV and GCD tests had set in the range from 0 to 1.0 V (vs. SCE). A linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 were used for current-voltage (I-V) measurements. In order to evaluate the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors in practical applications, two-electrode systems were used for CV and GCD tests. The symmetrical supercapacitor is prepared as follows (1) the electrodes were composed of 10 wt. % polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder, 10 wt. % acetylene black and 80 wt % as-synthesized hybrid; (2) the mixture smeared on a piece of round nickel foam (diameter 1.7 cm) with a loading of ca. 5.0 mg cm-2. After that, the nickel foam was dried in vacuum for 12 h at 60 °C and compressed by ca. 10 MPa for 5 min; (3) immerse the electrodes into the electrolyte for 5 min, and assembled as a button capacitor with cellulose separator. 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used for the electrolyte [38].

The influence of MF on the synthesis of CDs@PANI-0.5 T can be easily observed by the SEM and TEM results. Fig. 1 shows an obvious induction of MF in the synthesis of the polymer. The MF lines aligned the PANI main chain molecules in direction of field lines and finally formed a tube-like structure in both CDs@PANI-0.5 T (Fig. 1a) and PANI-0.5 T (Fig. S2a), whereas the morphologies of CDs@PANI-0 T (Fig. 1b) and PANI-0 T (Fig. S2b) samples are random and irregular clusters. Fig. 1a shows the SEM image of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs while Fig. 1c illustrates the magnified SEM image of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs with a marked area exposing the cross-section of a broken nanotube. To clearly inspect the PANI NTs, Fig. 1d shows a representative TEM image of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs, revealing that CDs are uniformly doped into the PANI NTs. The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1d) shows an individual carbon dot on a nanotube, in which the lattice spacing of carbon dot has a value of 0.216 nm that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite [44]. Additionally, Fig S3a indicates that the CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs are 70∼80 nm in the outer diameter and ca. 30 nm in the inner diameter, the maximum length is around 10 μm, which means an aspect ratio larger than 120 can be achieved for these NTs [39].

For comparison with the CDs-doped PANI samples, control samples without CDs doping (PANI-0.5 T and PANI-0 T) have also been synthesized by a similar way and their morphologies were examined by using SEM and TEM. The PANI-0 T (see Figs. S2b, S3d) exhibits random cluster-like comprised of irregular nanofibers, after the incorporation of tiny CDs, CDs@PANI-0 T did not change the morphology (see Figs. 2 b, S3b). However, both PANI-0.5 T and CDs@PANI-0.5 T samples synthesized under 0.5 T static MF have changed their shape remarkably (see Figs. S2a and Fig. 1a). That’s probably because, under the external MF induction, the conductive polymer tends to align more regularly and then forms hollow tube-like structures [40,41].

By XRD measurement, the results give the evidence that the MF can improve the crystallinity of PANI-based binary hybrid. A characteristic peak at 19.3° in the XRD patterns (Fig. 2a) confirms that the crystallinity of PANI-0.5 T and CDs@PANI-0.5 T has indeed improved rather than that of PANI-0 T and CDs@PANI-0 T [42]. The chemical structures of the CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, PANI-0 T, and CDs were investigated by Raman spectra. Fig. 2b shows two Raman spectra peaks at 1391 and 1560 cm−1 of all four polymeric samples, corresponding to the B and Q structure of PANI, respectively [41,45]. The characteristic peak at 1340 cm−1 can be ascribed to the band of PANI, which has been demonstrated by other reports [46,47]. The ratio of I B/I Q in the PANI-0 T spectrum is calculated to be 0.99, and larger than that of PANI-0.5 T (0.89), which means the polymer synthesized under MF contains more quinone structure. That’s because polymer synthesis under MF, the additional magnetic energy makes polymer tend to own higher Gibbs free energy, lower entropy, and more regular conjugated structure [43,48]. Moreover, the I B/I Q ratio of CDs@PANI-0.5 T and CDs@PANI-0 T in the Raman spectra are 0.89 and 0.96, respectively, which reveals that quinone structure of the polymer prepared under MF was increased, and the crystallinity of the system was also improved, which has been demonstrated by XRD (Fig. 2a) and TGA data (Fig. S4, Table S1).

The GCD curves were measured in a 3-electrode system (Fig. 3 a) with 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution as an electrolyte. The reason why we used Na2SO4 electrolyte other than strong acidic electrolytes for the CDs@PANI has been explained in Supplementary Information (Figs. S6-S8). GCD curves, the specific capacitances of the active material can calculate (C s) by the following equation [45–47]:
(1)
C s
=
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
Where m, ΔV, Δt, and I denote the mass of active electrode material, the potential window, discharge time and the discharge current, respectively. According to Eq. (1), the calculated C s of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 is 609, 485, 451, and 316 F g−1, respectively. In a three-electrode test system, CDs@PANI-0.5 T has a higher value of C s compared with those of PANI-0.5 T and CDs@PANI-0 T. The different current densities GCD curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T are shown in Fig. 3b. The C s of the CDs@PANI-0.5 T is calculated to be 609, 577, 518, 456, and 386 F g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10.0 A g−1, respectively. As the current density increases, the CDs@PANI-0.5 T gives a lower specific capacitance, implying that the adsorption of electrolyte cations on the electrode directly reduced in the fast charging and discharging processes.

Fig. 3c presents CV curves of the electrode materials at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in a 3-electrode system. Usually, caused by the resistive losses of EDLCs, a rectangular-shaped ideal EDLC CV plot changes to a parallelogram shape. The charge stored in the capacitor depends on the potential in Faradaic electrodes; therefore, the voltammetry characteristics of pseudo-capacitors deviated from redox and parallelogram peaks. As shown in Fig. 3c, compared to PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T electrodes; the increase of the pseudocapacitance on CV curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T materials is much better than the others, which means that the CDs@PANI-0.5 T electrolyte based cell will obtain much higher capacitance. It also confirms that the hybrid electrode made of CDs@PANI-0.5 T hybrid presence pseudo-capacitance. Additionally, at the working voltage of 1.0 V, CDs@PANI-0.5 T have shown an excellent and stable capacitive behavior from CV curve at different scan rates (Fig. 3d).

Doping CDs into PANI can promote the electron mobility of the conjugated system, and thus the dc conductivity of PANI is lower than that of CDs@PANI. As shown in Fig. 3e, CDs@PANI-0.5 T has a better conductivity than that of CDs@PANI-0 T. Table S2 has listed BET surface areas of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T and PANI-0 T samples. Obviously, the introduction of MF has made the specific surface area of CDs@PANI-0.5 T (35.84 m2 g−1) a little larger than those of CDs@PANI-0 T (32.16 m2 g−1), PANI-0 T (31.51 m2 g−1), and PANI-0.5 T (32.36 m2 g−1). Even though the specific surface area of CDs@PANI-0.5 T and PANI-0.5 T has almost the same, but C s of the former (609 F g−1) is almost 1.5-fold greater than the latter (485 F g−1) at the current of 0.5 A g−1. These data show that not only the surface area, there are some other factors which can also improve the capacitance, for example, reduced resistance of the electrode material due to CDs-enhanced electron transportation. And the incorporation of the CDs with abundant −COOH and -NH2 surface groups into PANI can provide more surface active sites for full access to electrolyte Na+ adsorption/desorption.

EIS revealed the electronic conductivity in redox processes. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 3f) forms an arc before a straight line in high frequency. The equivalent series resistance (R s) is represented by the x-axis high-frequency intercept, which is related to the sum of the resistance of the electrolyte solution, the contact resistance of the electrode/electrolyte interface and the intrinsic resistance of the active layer. In lower frequency, the straight line presents the diffusion behavior of ions in the electrode pores. But in the high frequency region, the diameter of the semicircle can be used to calculate the charge transfer resistance of electrode (R ct). In an ideal capacitive behavior, the steeper shape of the sloped line represents the strong diffusion of ions in electrolyte. Because a strong electrolyte (0.5 M Na2SO4) was employed, R s of PANI-0 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, PANI-0.5 T, and CDs@PANI-0.5 T is found to be as low as 4.17 Ω, 2.97 Ω, 2.48 Ω, and 1.42 Ω, respectively. In the equal solution-phase resistance in all EIS testing, as the same electrolyte was used, the lowest R s value of CDs@PANI-0.5 T indicates that its electrode internal resistance is lower than those of PANI-0 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0.5 T. In electrochemical systems, the semicircle diameter characterizes the charge transfer resistance (R ct) in the high frequency. The R ct of PANI-0 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, PANI-0.5 T, and CDs@PANI-0.5 T is calculated to be 8.9 Ω, 7.1 Ω, 5.4 Ω, and 2.6 Ω, respectively. Obviously, the R ct value of the CDs@PANI-0.5 T was smallest among these four electrode materials. The lowest value of R ct further proves that the hollow tube structure of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs can help the electrolyte ions access the inner layer and penetrate into the electrode active layer more easily. On the other hand, electron transfer from PANI to CDs has also contributed to decreasing the charge transfer resistance of CDs@PANI-0.5 T, which agrees with the results of Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) and I-V curves (Fig. 3e). Fig. S5 supports the electron transfer from PANI to CDs, in the room temperature red shift in the PL peaks of CDs@PANI-0.5 T (from 331.1 to 367.5 nm) and CDs@PANI-0 T (from 331.1 to 399.2 nm) are credited to the enhancement of electron mobility held by the CDs [31]. In general, the EIS testing has illustrated that CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs possess a charge transfer resistance and reduced ion diffusion path.

In a typical EDLC capacitance, the electrode-electrolyte interface charge accumulated has generally determined by Eq. (2) [2]:
(2)
C =
ε 0
ε r
A D
Where D is the distance between the two electrodes, A is the area of overlap, ε r is the dielectric constant of an electrolyte, and ε 0 is the permittivity of free space. Here, the increase of the surface area of an active layer comprised of CDs@PANI-0.5 T NTs may increase its EDLC capacitance effectively. In addition, CDs@PANI-0.5 T shows larger dielectric permittivity (ε′) than those of PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T and PANI-0 T in the range of 2∼18 GHz (Fig. 4 ), ε′ is the real part of permittivity and usually represents the dielectric constant of material under ac condition. On the capacitance of the supercapacitors, the influence of increased dielectric constant of the electrode active materials is not very clear, yet the easier polarization and hence the improved charge storage may be supposed.

The four samples were cycled for 2000 times to estimate their cycle stability at 10.0 A g−1 (Fig. 5 a). The capacitance retention of PANI-0 T (68.67%) became lower than those of CDs@PANI-0 T (71.86%), PANI-0.5 T (79.06%) and CDs@PANI-0.5 T (85.19%) after 1000 cycles. After 2000 cycles, the stability of CDs@PANI-0.5 T has a significant increase in capacitance retention of 76.51%, a comparison of the low capacitance retention of PANI-0 T (56.37%), CDs@PANI-0 T (58.28%) and PANI-0.5 T (68.03%). Similar to most of the hollow structured electrode materials, CDs@PANI-0.5 T has hollow and long tube-like structure, which not only avoid the structural collapse caused by charge storage/delivery and keep the stability of electrode but also can help fast ion transfer between the electrolyte and the electrode [15].

By utilizing the electric charge ratio of the GCD, the Coulombic efficiency of the electrode can be easily calculated. The Coulombic efficiency of CDs@PANI-0.5 T has increased to 98% after 372 cycles and remains unchanged until 2000 cycles (Fig. 5b). The Coulombic efficiencies of controlled samples PANI-0 T, CDs@PANI-0 T and PANI-0.5 T have also increased to 97% after 707 cycles, to 98% after 607 cycles and to 99% after 406 cycles, respectively. After 2000 cycles, the GCD curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T becomes linear, which evidence that the CDs@PANI-0.5 T with 0.5 mol L−1 aqueous Na2SO4 solution exhibits an excellent and stable capacitance behavior under charge-discharge cycles. Therefore, the excellent cycle stability as well as high Coulombic efficiency reveal the uniformity and high quality of the CDs@PANI-0.5 T electrodes in symmetrical supercapacitor.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, specific capacitance of assembled supercapacitor (C cell) have been measured from GCD curves by utilizing the following equation [47]:
(3)
C cell
=
I × Δ t
m total
× Δ V
Where ΔV, m total, Δt, and I denote the potential window, the sample mass of the two electrodes, discharge time, and the discharge current, respectively. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the C cell of CDs@PANI-0.5 T (404.5 F g−1) is higher than those of PANI-0.5 T (351.3 F g−1), CDs@PANI-0 T (302.7 F g−1), and PANI-0 T (243.6 F g−1). As for CDs@PANI-0.5 T, C cell of the fabricated symmetric supercapacitor are evaluated as 212.5, 290.3, 332.4, 361.7, and 404.5 F g−1 at current densities of 10, 5.0, 2.0, 1.0, and 0.5 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 6b). In a low scan rate (10 mV s−1), CDs@PANI-0.5 T owns larger-area rectangular CV curves and shows a better electrochemical performance than others. The CV curves of CDs@PANI-0.5 T at scan rates of 100, 50, 20, 10, and 5 mV s−1 exhibited in Fig. 6d, respectively. The pseudocapacitance CV curve of CDs@PANI-0.5 T shows the capacitance behavior of an ideal supercapacitor.

The constant current discharge data can be used for the calculation of ragone plots (see Fig. 7 ), the specific power density (P m) and energy density (E m) of the capacitor can be calculated from the following equations [49]:
(4)
E m
=
1 2
×
C cell
× Δ
V 2
(5)
P m
=
E m
Δ t
Where Δt, ΔV, and C cell, denote the discharge time, the potential window and the specific capacitance of supercapacitor calculated from Eq. (3), respectively. From Eqs. (4) and (5), the maximum P m of CDs@PANI-0.5 T is 249.8 W kg−1 when the E m achieves 56.62 Wh kg−1. As the P m increases to 2000 W kg−1, the E m can still preserve 73.42%. For some references of PANI-based or PANI/RGO-based electrode materials, the obtained maximum E m in this work is higher in the same power density.

As shown in Fig. S9a, a small set of four supercapacitors assembled by connecting in series to further utilize for actual applications. The assembled device was charged up to 4.0 V at 5.0 A g−1 for only 10 s, and then 59 light-emitting diodes (LED, 3 mm diameter) lightened by the assembled device (see Fig. S6b) for more than 40 s.

A novel hybrid CDs-doped PANI NTs synthesized under 0.5 T magnetic field (CDs@PANI-0.5 T) with a prominent electrochemical performance is reported. The applied MF can improve the morphology, crystallinity, and conductivity of PANI nanotubes. The hollow structure of nanotube has a great contribution to improve the cycle stability and electronic transmission of the supercapacitor electrode. The doped CDs can increase the electron mobility of the PANI NTs, which eventually reduce both the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and internal resistance (R s) of the electrode materials, and enhance the specific capacitance of the electrodes. In fact, CDs@PANI-0.5 T shows a superior specific capacitance (609 F g−1) at 0.5 A g−1 to control samples of PANI-0.5 T, CDs@PANI-0 T, and PANI-0 T. Symmetric supercapacitor assembled by the CDs@PANI-0.5 T-based electrodes shows a high power density of 249.8 W kg−1 at an energy density (56.62 Wh kg−1) as well as a good stability after 2000 charge/discharge cycles. In practice, a simple device is assembled from four super capacitors in series, which can power 59 LEDs for more than 40 s after charging for only 10 s. This work can provide us with a new pathway to design and fabricate flexible, stable and high-performance supercapacitors at a commercial level based on a hybrid of carbon and conducting polymers.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledged the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51772001), Key Laboratory of Environmental-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Key Project of Natural Science Research of University in Anhui Provence (No. KJ2016A577) and Anhui Provincial Key Scientific and Technological Project (No. 1604a0902178) for providing funds for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2018.09.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
